title: IT Support Specialist
insight: AI helpdesk agents now resolve most tier-1 tickets -- resets, installs, routing -- without a human touch, and that volume was this role's core. The specialists still hired are the ones running and tuning those agents, or moving into security first-response and cloud identity work.

title: Systems Administrator
insight: Cloud automation and AI-ops tooling now run provisioning, patching, and backup verification without a human in the loop. The clearest path forward is cloud architecture, security hardening, and automation design rather than day-to-day server administration.

title: Business Intelligence Analyst
defense_skill_1: Directing AI BI Copilots (Power BI, Looker)
insight: AI BI copilots now build and refresh most recurring dashboards without an analyst touching the query. The role's future is in deciding what should be measured in the first place and chasing down why a metric moved, not in maintaining the pipeline that reports it.

title: Network Support Specialist
insight: AI triage tools now resolve most known-fix network tickets before a specialist sees them. The path forward is running that triage layer and handling the multi-site, security-flavored incidents it can't close on its own.

title: QA/Test Engineer
insight: Agentic test tools now write, run, and self-heal most regression suites, which is why routine QA is now a substantially agent-owned workflow. Engineers who validate AI system outputs and own security/performance test strategy are the ones still growing in value.

title: Data Analyst
defense_skill_1: Directing AI BI Copilots (Power BI, Tableau, Looker)
insight: AI BI copilots now write the queries and refresh the dashboards that used to be most of this job. Analysts who frame the business question and tell the story behind the numbers are pulling away from analysts who just build reports.

title: Database Administrator
defense_skill_2: Vector Database & RAG Data Layer Management
insight: Autonomous cloud databases now handle tuning, patching, and backup verification without a human in the loop, which is why routine DBA work is the most automated slice of the Technology category. The growth path is data architecture for AI workloads, vector data layers, and security -- not day-to-day administration.

title: Web Developer
insight: AI page-builders now ship standard sites and components with little human editing. Developers who own complex application logic, accessibility, and AI feature integration are the ones still commanding a premium.

title: DevOps Engineer
insight: AI now writes and maintains most CI/CD configuration and IaC boilerplate directly. Engineers who move into platform strategy, incident command, and security architecture are the ones whose value keeps climbing as the routine plumbing disappears.

title: Data Engineer
insight: AI now writes and self-heals most routine pipeline code, from ETL scripts to data-quality checks. The lasting work is architecting data platforms for AI training and RAG workloads, where governance and lineage decisions still need a person accountable for them.

title: Mobile Developer
insight: AI now scaffolds standard screens and common features for both major mobile platforms directly from a spec. Developers who own cross-platform architecture and can debug real device fragmentation issues are the ones clients still need.

title: Site Reliability Engineer
insight: Agents now run first-pass triage and execute known runbooks before a human is paged. The SREs still in demand are the ones who own reliability architecture and can run incident command when the runbook doesn't cover it.

title: UX Designer
defense_skill_3: Directing AI Design Tools (Figma AI, Galileo)
insight: AI can generate mockups and component variations fast, but understanding real user needs and designing for AI-human interaction remain judgment calls a model can't make on its own.

title: AI Engineer
insight: The tooling this role builds with is itself good enough to automate a meaningful share of its own scaffolding -- prompt templates, RAG wiring, eval harnesses. Engineers who own guardrail design and multi-agent orchestration for genuinely novel workflows are the ones the market is still short of.

title: Platform Engineer
insight: AI now maintains a lot of the golden-path templates and CI/CD scaffolding platform teams used to hand-tend. The differentiator is driving adoption across teams and making the standardization-versus-autonomy tradeoff correctly, which no template can decide.

title: Data Scientist
insight: Agentic notebooks can now run an entire clean-model-report loop on structured data unattended, which pushes routine modeling toward a commodity task. Data scientists who frame the right problem, design the experiment, and translate results into a business decision remain the ones firms pay for.

title: Machine Learning Engineer
insight: Training pipelines and hyperparameter sweeps are now largely agent-run, but production risk sign-off and novel failure-mode debugging still require an engineer who understands the model, not just the framework. The premium is shifting toward engineers who can evaluate and govern what agents ship.

title: Security Engineer
insight: Automated remediation now closes most known vulnerability classes without an engineer touching a ticket. The differentiator is security architecture for genuinely new systems -- including the AI applications the rest of the org is shipping.

title: Software Engineer
defense_skill_1: Directing Agentic Coding Tools (Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot Workspace)
insight: AI coding agents now handle scaffolding, tests, and routine bug fixes end-to-end, but architecture, security tradeoffs, and cross-team judgment still gate the work that matters. The role is shifting from writing every line to directing and verifying what agents produce.

title: Cybersecurity Analyst
insight: AI SOC copilots now clear most tier-1 alert volume, but they also expand the attack surface faster than they close it. Analysts who supervise agentic triage and specialize in AI-native threats -- prompt injection, model poisoning -- are pulling ahead of generalists.

title: Game Developer
insight: AI now writes standard gameplay systems code and can generate a fair share of routine assets, but the creative judgment behind what makes a game actually feel good to play is still squarely a human skill.

title: Network Engineer
insight: AI can now generate standard router and switch configurations from a template, but multi-vendor network design and diagnosing an unfamiliar topology under outage pressure still need an engineer who has seen the failure mode before.

title: Blockchain Developer
insight: AI can now scaffold a standard smart contract quickly, which makes security auditing -- where a missed edge case costs real money on-chain -- the part of this role that is actually growing in value.

title: Cloud Architect
insight: AI can draft a reference architecture and the IaC to match it, but choosing between vendors, owning the cost/resilience tradeoff, and getting a regulated workload through security review still take an accountable architect.

title: Database Architect
insight: AI can draft a reasonable schema for a well-understood problem, but the decisions that actually matter -- how data flows across systems, who governs it, and how it serves both transactional and AI workloads -- still need an accountable architect.

title: Solutions Architect
insight: AI now handles the first draft of the diagram and the proposal document. What still requires a person is sitting across from a client, understanding what they actually need, and owning the tradeoff decision when the textbook answer doesn't fit their constraints.

title: AI Research Scientist
defense_skill_1: Agentic Experiment Pipelines & Self-Driving Research Loops
insight: Agentic experiment loops are starting to run their own ablation studies and literature synthesis, but the research direction and theoretical insight that make a result matter still come from a scientist, not the loop.

title: Embedded Systems Engineer
insight: This is one of the least agent-exposed engineering roles today: AI tooling for low-level, hardware-coupled firmware still lags mainstream software copilots badly, and physical hardware-in-the-loop debugging keeps a person firmly in the loop.

title: IT Manager
defense_skill_3: Team Leadership Through an AI Transition
insight: AI now drafts the status reports and vendor summaries that used to eat a manager's week, but leading a team and deciding where to invest a shrinking headcount budget is still an entirely human call.

title: Computer Hardware Engineer
insight: AI-assisted simulation and EDA tools can speed up routine schematic work, but hardware design still lives or dies in physical prototyping and lab validation that no model can substitute for.

title: Tax Preparer
insight: AI tax software now files the majority of standard individual returns with minimal human input, and the segment keeps shrinking. Preparers who specialize in multi-state, international, or business tax planning are the ones with a growing practice left.

title: Insurance Underwriter
defense_skill_1: AI risk modeling and insurtech platforms
insight: AI now underwrites the overwhelming majority of standard personal lines with no human touch. The career path forward is complex commercial risk, emerging categories like cyber and AI liability, and broker relationships that carriers still need a person to run.

title: Bank Teller
defense_skill_1: Complex product cross-selling and financial guidance
insight: Branch teller headcount has been shrinking for over a decade as mobile and ATM/ITM channels absorb routine transactions, and AI-driven self-service accelerates that shift further.

title: Loan Officer
insight: Online lenders now originate most standard consumer loans with essentially no loan officer involvement. Officers who build deep client relationships and master complex commercial or construction lending remain the ones borrowers and banks still need.

title: Accountant
defense_skill_1: AI accounting platforms (Vic.ai, Botkeeper, QBO AI)
insight: Routine bookkeeping, reconciliation, and standard tax prep are now largely agent-run rather than merely assisted. Accountants who move up into advisory, complex multi-entity tax strategy, or forensic work keep pricing power; those who stay in pure transaction processing compete directly with software.

title: Bookkeeper
defense_skill_1: AI bookkeeping platform administration (Botkeeper, QBO AI)
insight: Bookkeeping is one of the most fully agent-automatable roles in finance today: categorization, reconciliation, and reporting now run with minimal human input. The path forward is overseeing the AI, handling exceptions, and moving into client-facing advisory work the software can't do.

title: Auditor
defense_skill_2: Data analytics for audit (ACL, IDEA, continuous audit tools)
insight: Full-population continuous auditing has moved from pilot to default at large firms, replacing sample-based testing outright. Auditors who specialize in judgment-heavy risk assessment, AI-system auditing, or fraud analytics are the ones clients still pay a premium for.

title: Financial Analyst
insight: AI now builds the model and pulls the data in minutes; the analysts who keep their seat are the ones translating the output into a judgment call an executive can act on under real uncertainty.

title: Budget Analyst
insight: AI now closes the variance-report-to-forecast loop with minimal input, but budget analysts who can advise leadership on trade-offs and broker cross-department negotiation still set the agenda.

title: Claims Adjuster
insight: Touchless claims already settle most straightforward auto and property losses without a human adjuster. The remaining career path is in complex, contested, and catastrophic claims where judgment and negotiation still decide the outcome.

title: Business Analyst
defense_skill_1: AI process automation and agent orchestration
insight: AI now drafts requirements and pulls reports directly from meetings and data, but reading organizational politics and redesigning workflows around AI remain distinctly human work.

title: Credit Analyst
defense_skill_1: Complex commercial credit and covenant analysis
insight: Algorithmic scoring already handles most consumer and small-business credit decisions. Analysts who move into complex commercial deals and portfolio-level risk strategy keep the work software can't yet do.

title: Compliance Officer
insight: AI regulation is exploding worldwide, and monitoring/checklist work is automating fast, but every one of those new rules needs a human who can interpret it and build the internal case for compliance. AI-governance specialists are the fastest-growing niche in the field.

title: Fraud Investigator
defense_skill_3: Emerging fraud typology analysis (deepfake, synthetic ID)
insight: AI now catches the overwhelming majority of anomalies automatically, which is actually growing the investigator role: someone still has to confirm intent, interview the subject, and build a case that holds up, and fraud volume keeps climbing.

title: Financial Advisor
insight: Robo-advisors have commoditized basic portfolio management, but the advisors who keep and grow books of business are the ones clients call during a market crash or a life event, not the ones who just rebalance a portfolio.

title: Real Estate Appraiser
insight: Appraisal-waiver programs and automated valuation models are eroding standard-appraisal volume steadily, but licensed sign-off on complex or disputed properties still requires a certified human on site.

title: Management Consultant
insight: AI now handles research and deck-building faster than any analyst team, but clients still pay for judgment, relationships, and someone accountable for the outcome. Consultants who specialize in AI transformation itself are in peak demand.

title: Credit Counselor
insight: Debt-plan math is easy to automate, but people in financial distress come to a credit counselor for a person who will negotiate on their behalf and help them change behavior, not a calculator.

title: Real Estate Agent
insight: AI now writes the listing and pulls the comps, but buying and selling a home is still an emotional, high-stakes, in-person negotiation. Agents who lean into local expertise and relationship management outcompete discount and AI-only listing models.

title: Financial Risk Specialist
defense_skill_1: AI/ML model risk validation and governance
insight: Regulators now treat AI/ML model risk as a top priority, which is turning financial risk specialists into the certified check on every automated pricing and credit model a bank deploys.

title: Financial Examiner
insight: Examination data review is getting faster with AI, but the statutory finding and enforcement decision stay with a certified examiner, and new risk categories like crypto and AI-driven lending are expanding the job, not shrinking it.

title: Actuary
defense_skill_2: Predictive modeling for novel risk (climate, cyber)
insight: Actuarial work is professionally licensed and carries personal liability for the certified opinion, which keeps AI in an assistive role even as modeling tools improve quickly. Actuaries who also validate AI/ML risk models are becoming the bridge between data science and regulatory-grade certification.

title: Purchasing Manager
insight: AI now surfaces spend patterns and reorder points automatically, but negotiating supplier terms and managing a sourcing team under real supply-chain risk stays a human judgment call.

title: Economist
defense_skill_1: Original research design and causal inference
insight: AI speeds up the literature review and the first regression, but the value of an economist is still in the research question and the judgment call about what the numbers actually mean for policy.

title: Investment Banker
defense_skill_1: Deal origination and client relationship management
insight: AI now drafts the pitch book in a fraction of the time, but the job has always really been about winning and closing the mandate, which is still a trust-and-negotiation business.

title: Telemarketer
insight: Scripted cold calling is now close to a pure agent job: an AI voice agent dials, pitches, and logs outcomes at a fraction of the cost. The remaining human seats sit in regulated products and campaigns where a compliance disclosure or an angry prospect needs a person on the line, and even those seats are shrinking as call-center operators consolidate onto voice-agent platforms.

title: Content Writer
insight: Generic drafting is now a commodity an agent can run start to finish across a full content calendar. Writers left standing are the ones who bring reporting AI can't originate, subject expertise it can't fake, and editorial judgment over what an agent produces.

title: Email Marketing Specialist
insight: Build, test, and send is now a loop an agent runs unattended inside modern ESPs. Specialists who own deliverability health and revenue attribution across the lifecycle stay indispensable.

title: Media Buyer
insight: Bid pacing and reporting now run almost entirely inside AI-managed DSPs. The remaining work is negotiating the deals platforms can't auto-execute and catching the fraud and brand-safety issues an algorithm misses.

title: SEO Specialist
insight: Audits and keyword clustering now run on agents, and the audience is shifting from search results to AI answers. Specialists who master AI-search visibility and can still touch the technical stack remain in demand.

title: Copywriter
insight: Catalog and variant copy is now an agent job, run and A/B-tested at volumes no human team could match. Copywriters who survive own the brand voice an agent works from and the high-stakes launch copy nobody trusts it to write alone.

title: Growth Marketer
insight: The build-launch-measure experiment loop is now something an agent runs continuously. Growth marketers who pick the right bets and read ambiguous results correctly are the ones still worth the seat.

title: Social Media Manager
insight: Posting, captioning, and basic reporting now run on agents around the clock. Managers who own real-time community trust and crisis response are the ones a brand still can't automate away.

title: Market Research Analyst
insight: Tabulating data and tracking competitors is now agent work. Analysts who ask the sharper research question and translate findings into a strategic story for leadership are the ones that stay in the room.

title: PR Specialist
insight: Routine releases and coverage tracking now run themselves. What still needs a person under pressure is a crisis response, a real relationship with a reporter, and a narrative built to survive scrutiny.

title: Advertising Manager
insight: Bidding, targeting, and creative rotation now run themselves inside agentic ad platforms. Advertising managers who still own the budget strategy, agency relationships, and the creative narrative across channels are the ones platforms can't route around.

title: Marketing Manager
insight: Execution and reporting now run largely on autopilot. Marketing managers who own positioning, customer insight, and orchestration across an ever-multiplying set of channels are becoming more valuable, not less.

title: Sales Representative
insight: Prospecting and pipeline admin now run largely on agents. Reps who win are the ones clients trust to understand a nuanced problem and negotiate a complex deal, which no agent closes alone yet.

title: Customer Success Manager
insight: Low-touch onboarding and renewal reminders now run on automated playbooks, which is exactly why headcount is under pressure at SaaS companies in 2026. CSMs who survive own the enterprise accounts where an escalation or expansion conversation still needs a trusted human relationship.

title: Account Executive
insight: Prospecting and pipeline admin now run largely on AI SDR agents. What still requires a human is reading the room across a multi-stakeholder buying committee and closing a deal a prospect has to trust.

title: Insurance Sales Agent
insight: Personal-lines insurance is already sold by software; agents surviving the shift are advisors on complex risk, not quote processors.

title: Wholesale Sales Representative
insight: Reordering and catalog lookups are moving to self-service B2B portals. Reps who keep the relationship that wins shelf space and negotiates volume pricing are harder to route around.

title: Brand Manager
insight: AI now handles the competitive tracking and documentation that used to eat a brand manager's week. The job increasingly is positioning strategy and creative judgment, which is exactly what AI can't originate.

title: Retail Salesperson
insight: Self-checkout and inventory tools automate the paperwork, but the floor interaction that actually closes a sale is still an in-person, physical job AI doesn't reach yet.

title: Product Demonstrator
insight: This is one of the most physically-grounded, in-person roles in marketing, and it stays that way: AI can write the script, but it can't stand at the sample table and make the pitch.

title: Data Entry Clerk
defense_skill_1: AI Document Intelligence & OCR Pipeline Configuration
insight: Document AI and agentic extraction pipelines now handle the full capture-validate-file loop for structured data, leaving almost no defensible core to this role as historically specified. The realistic path is moving into the exception queue and the pipeline-configuration work that keeps the automation running.

title: Administrative Assistant
defense_skill_1: Agentic AI Workflow Orchestration (Copilot, Gemini agents)
insight: Agentic assistants now run the full scheduling-to-inbox-to-expense loop with minimal supervision, which is why routine admin work keeps climbing toward Very High risk. The defensible ground is judgment-heavy coordination — reading a room, managing competing executive priorities, catching what an agent gets subtly wrong — not any single software skill.

title: Call Center Agent
insight: AI voice agents now run the bulk of routine call center volume end-to-end, which is why this role carries the steepest agentic exposure in the category. Agents who move into escalation handling, retention calls, and AI-voice-agent supervision are the ones still needed on the floor.

title: Payroll Specialist
insight: Standard payroll runs are now close to fully automated by platforms with built-in compliance engines, pushing this role toward High risk. The remaining ground is genuinely hard compliance — multi-state, cross-border, system-migration work — where a mistake is expensive enough that employers still want a specialist.

title: Receptionist
insight: AI voice attendants and check-in kiosks now handle most routine front-desk traffic, which is why this role sits at High risk. The remaining value is in-person: reading a nervous visitor, handling a security issue, making a VIP feel looked after — things a kiosk can't do.

title: Customer Service Representative
insight: AI chat and voice agents now close a majority of tier-1 tickets without a human touch, which is why routine customer service sits at High risk. The remaining seats go to reps who handle the escalations an agent bounces back — angry customers, ambiguous multi-product problems, and judgment calls the bot won't make.

title: Order Clerk
insight: AI order-management systems now take standard orders from intake to confirmation without a human, which is why this role sits at High risk. Clerks handling discrepancies and custom configurations hold the more defensible ground.

title: Executive Assistant
insight: AI now assembles the calendar, drafts the emails, and preps the briefing deck; what it can't do is read a principal's unstated priorities or manage the politics around them. EAs who move toward chief-of-staff-style trusted advising hold their ground better than those who stay purely logistical.

title: Inventory Clerk
insight: Barcode, RFID, and computer-vision counting have automated most routine stock tracking, pushing this role toward High risk.

title: Records Clerk
insight: AI document-management systems now file, tag, and retrieve most records automatically, pushing this role to High risk. Clerks who move into records compliance and legal-hold work hold a more defensible position than pure filing and retrieval.

title: Billing Specialist
insight: AI billing platforms now generate invoices and chase routine payments automatically, which pushes this role to High risk. The durable work is disputed charges and negotiated arrangements where a system alone can't close the case.

title: Office Clerk (General)
defense_skill_2: AI Office-Suite Power Use (Copilot, Gemini for Workspace)
insight: One of America's largest occupations sits squarely in the path of document AI and office agents; clerks who become the person who runs and audits the automation keep the job.

title: Logistics Coordinator
insight: Tracking, routing, and standard rate comparison are now largely automated by TMS copilots, but real supply chains still break in ways only a person can untangle. Coordinators who specialize in disruption response and carrier relationships are more durable than those doing pure status-update work.

title: Office Manager
insight: Ordering supplies and tracking a budget are now largely automatable, but running an office still means reading a room and fixing whatever nobody planned for. Office managers who take on real cross-functional operations leadership are outgrowing the role's clerical reputation.

title: Procurement Specialist
insight: AI now handles the transactional half of procurement — POs, catalog pricing, invoice matching — leaving negotiation and supplier risk as the durable core. Specialists who move up into strategic sourcing hold a clearly defensible High-but-not-Very-High position.

title: Mail Clerk
insight: Scan-and-distribute systems now handle a growing share of digital mail routing, but someone still has to physically sort packages and hand-deliver sensitive documents. That keeps this role at Moderate risk rather than pushing it higher.

title: Supply Chain Analyst
defense_skill_1: AI Supply Chain Forecasting & Optimization Tools
insight: Forecasting and inventory math are now largely AI-generated starting points, but building a network that survives a real disruption still takes an analyst who understands the business, not just the model. That keeps this role Moderate rather than sliding toward High.

title: Facilities Manager
defense_skill_1: Smart Building IoT & Predictive Maintenance Platforms
insight: AI now triages work orders and flags maintenance needs before a person notices, but someone still has to stand in the mechanical room, manage the contractor, and own the safety call. Facilities managers who add sustainability and smart-building program leadership to core operations are growing in scope, not shrinking.

title: Operations Manager
insight: AI now compiles the reports and drafts the schedules an operations manager used to build by hand, but leading a team through an ambiguous, high-stakes day still needs a person in the room. That is why this role stays firmly Low risk even as the reporting work underneath it automates.

title: Warehouse Manager
insight: AI now handles the scheduling math and throughput reporting, but running a physical floor safely still takes a person who can read the room and react to a forklift breakdown. That keeps this role solidly Low risk.

title: Medical Transcriptionist
insight: Ambient AI scribes now draft the majority of clinical notes directly from the patient encounter, collapsing the separate transcription step almost entirely. The remaining career path runs through documentation-quality oversight, not typing.

title: Medical Coder
insight: AI coding engines now assign codes for routine single-diagnosis visits with production-grade accuracy, and agentic pipelines are starting to run the full code-verify-submit loop with a human auditing exceptions. Coders who specialize in complex multi-comorbidity cases and own that audit layer stay essential.

title: Pharmacy Technician
insight: Robotic dispensing and automated insurance adjudication now run routine fills close to end to end, which is why this is the most exposed role in the healthcare category. Technicians who move into compounding or automation-system oversight have the clearer path forward.

title: Pharmacist
insight: Robotic dispensing and AI interaction-checking now run routine fills close to end to end, with a pharmacist reviewing exceptions. Pharmacists moving into clinical consultation and medication-therapy management are seeing their scope grow, not shrink.

title: Radiologist
insight: AI now triages and pre-screens most normal-study volume and drafts routine reports, compressing read time on straightforward cases. The complex, ambiguous and interventional work that defines the specialty still sits with the radiologist.

title: Healthcare Administrator
insight: Agentic tools now run scheduling, billing triage and standard compliance reports end to end with light oversight, cutting the back-office headcount these functions once required. Administrators who lead AI implementation and own regulatory and quality strategy are taking on more scope, not less.

title: Lab Technician
insight: Automated analyzers and AI-based QC flagging now handle the bulk of routine testing volume end to end, but someone still has to load, prep and troubleshoot the physical specimen path. Techs who manage lab automation systems and validate new assays keep growing scope.

title: Dietitian
insight: Consumer apps now generate meal plans and track macros about as well as a routine consult would, pushing dietitians toward medical nutrition therapy and behavior-change counseling for complex conditions. The generic meal-planning end of the field is the most exposed.

title: Medical Assistant
insight: AI intake and scheduling tools are absorbing a real share of front-desk and documentation work in clinics. Medical assistants who move toward hands-on clinical support and cross-train into higher-scope roles have the clearest path forward.

title: Medical Lab Scientist
insight: AI-based result flagging now catches most routine abnormalities automatically, but validating new assays and troubleshooting complex specimens still requires a trained scientist's judgment. Labs are shifting scientists toward automation oversight rather than routine testing.

title: Physician
insight: Ambient AI scribes and decision-support tools are cutting real documentation burden, and physicians who pair clinical judgment with these tools are becoming more effective, not less needed. The complex reasoning and procedures at the core of the job stay out of reach for now.

title: Nurse Practitioner
insight: Ambient scribing is cutting real documentation time for NPs the same way it is for physicians, and NPs are increasingly filling the primary-care access gap AI can't close. Scope-of-practice expansion is a bigger factor than AI displacement here.

title: Optometrist
insight: Autonomous AI systems already screen for diabetic retinopathy at close to specialist accuracy, and autorefractors handle routine prescriptions. Optometrists managing eye disease and complex fittings hold the ground AI hasn't reached.

title: Medical Sonographer
insight: AI now auto-populates standard measurements on many scans, but getting a diagnostic-quality image out of a moving probe against a moving patient is still a hands-on skill no model performs. Demand for sonographers keeps growing with imaging volume.

title: Physician Assistant
insight: PAs are absorbing the same ambient documentation gains as physicians and NPs, freeing time for exams and procedures rather than replacing them. Demand keeps climbing as health systems lean on PAs to extend physician capacity.

title: Speech-Language Pathologist
insight: AI apps now support standardized scoring and generate practice materials between sessions, but delivering therapy still requires a clinician adjusting technique in real time. School and healthcare demand for SLPs continues to outpace the training pipeline.

title: Veterinary Technician
insight: Veterinary tech work is hands-on animal care with a modest paperwork layer around it, and AI tooling for this specific role is still immature. Corporate consolidation and staffing shortages are bigger near-term forces than automation.

title: Psychiatrist
insight: AI drafts notes and tracks symptom scales, but diagnosing and treating mental illness still requires a licensed physician building trust with a patient over time. Demand for psychiatric care continues to outstrip the supply of psychiatrists.

title: Radiologic Technologist
insight: AI dose-optimization and image-QC tools are improving fast, but positioning a patient correctly for a diagnostic-quality scan is still a manual skill performed one patient at a time. Cross-training into CT and MRI modalities offers the clearest advancement path.

title: Veterinarian
insight: AI triage chatbots and imaging tools are starting to handle routine intake and screening in veterinary practices, but surgery and hands-on animal care remain squarely the veterinarian's. Corporate consolidation is a bigger near-term force on the job than AI is.

title: Mental Health Counselor
insight: AI now drafts session notes and scores standard screening tools, cutting real administrative load. What it can't do is sit with a person in crisis, and demand for that keeps outpacing supply.

title: Physical Therapist
insight: AI handles documentation and outcome tracking, freeing PTs for the hands-on treatment that actually drives recovery. Demand keeps growing faster than the pipeline of new therapists.

title: Nurse (RN)
insight: AI documentation assistants and monitoring alerts cut real charting time, but nursing's core, hands-on assessment, advocacy and rapid judgment at the bedside, sits outside what agents can own. The supply-demand gap keeps widening.

title: Dentist
insight: AI now flags caries and bone loss on X-rays before the dentist even looks, tightening diagnosis but not touching the drill. The procedures that define the job stay licensed, physical, and human for the foreseeable future.

title: Occupational Therapist
insight: AI now handles documentation and tracks recovery metrics, but functional rehabilitation is inherently hands-on work built around a specific patient's body and environment. Demand keeps climbing alongside an aging population and rising chronic-condition rates.

title: EMT/Paramedic
insight: Emergency response is in-person, time-critical physical work that AI can support at the margins, dispatch routing, report drafting, but not perform. Paramedic-level certification keeps opening more career ceiling than AI closes.

title: Surgeon
insight: AI assists with pre-op planning and drafts the paperwork around a case, but the operation itself is manual, liability-bearing work no model performs. Surgical robotics extend a surgeon's hands; they don't replace the surgeon.

title: Dental Hygienist
insight: Hygiene work is a licensed physical act performed in a patient's mouth, one of the least AI-exposed jobs in health care. The only automatable slice is scheduling and note templating around the appointment.

title: Phlebotomist
insight: Robotic venipuncture devices remain experimental, so the needle stick stays a manual, licensed act for the foreseeable future. The digital slice of this job, scheduling and logging, is small and already partly automated.

title: Home Health Aide
insight: This is about as AI-resistant as work gets: in-person personal care with no digital surface to automate. Demand is rising fast with an aging population, and the constraint is workforce supply, not AI displacement.

title: Contract Reviewer
defense_skill_1: AI Contract Analysis Tools (Kira, Ironclad, Ivo, Spellbook)
insight: AI contract-review tools now clear standard commercial paper in minutes and flag exceptions on their own. Reviewers who remain valuable are the ones who own the playbook, tune the AI, and handle the non-standard deal terms it can't yet judge.

title: Legal Secretary
insight: AI now handles document formatting, scheduling, and routine correspondence directly, cutting deep into the traditional job. Legal secretaries should move toward paralegal certification and legal-technology administration to stay ahead of it.

title: E-Discovery Specialist
defense_skill_1: TAR & Generative-AI Review Platform Mastery (Relativity aiR, Everlaw, DISCO)
insight: Technology-assisted review already does most first-pass document culling at a fraction of linear-review cost. Specialists who remain are defending review methodology in court and making the privilege calls AI still gets wrong.

title: Court Reporter
insight: Speech-to-text has closed most of the accuracy gap for routine depositions and hearings, and cost-pressured courts are piloting digital recording over stenographers. The remaining moat is the legally certified verbatim record: someone accountable for accuracy in complex, multi-speaker, or high-stakes proceedings.

title: Title Examiner
defense_skill_1: Automated Title-Search Platform Fluency (Qualia, Endpoint, title-plant AI)
insight: Automated title search now clears most standard residential resales in digitized counties end-to-end; examiners increasingly specialize in the contested, rural, or commercial titles automation still can't clear.

title: Paralegal
insight: AI now handles document review and standard research at scale on its own. Paralegals who master legal AI tools, specialize in complex practice areas, and manage technology-driven workflows will remain valuable.

title: Compliance Analyst
insight: AI now tracks regulatory changes and drafts compliance reports on its own, compressing the routine half of the job. Analysts who specialize in AI-specific regulation (EU AI Act, state AI laws) and cross-border complexity are moving into a high-growth niche instead.

title: Judicial Law Clerk
defense_skill_1: AI Legal Research Co-Piloting (CoCounsel, Harvey, Westlaw AI)
insight: AI research tools now produce a credible first-pass bench memo in minutes; clerks who remain valuable are the ones a judge trusts to catch what the memo got wrong and frame the actual judgment call.

title: Lawyer
defense_skill_1: AI Legal Research Tools (Westlaw AI, CoCounsel)
insight: AI accelerates research and drafting, making lawyers more productive rather than obsolete. Lawyers who master AI tools and specialize in AI-related law (IP, liability, regulation) are positioned for growth.

title: Administrative Law Judge
insight: Case-prep and record summarization are increasingly AI-assisted, especially in high-volume dockets like disability and unemployment hearings, but statute still requires a human officer to weigh credibility and issue the binding decision.

title: Mediator
insight: ODR platforms now clear routine e-commerce and small-claims disputes without a human in the loop, but complex mediation still runs on trust and reading a room, territory current agents don't credibly occupy.

title: Judge
defense_skill_1: AI-Assisted Judicial Research Oversight (CoCounsel, Westlaw AI)
defense_skill_3: Judicial Ethics & AI Governance in Court Technology
insight: AI now drafts bench memos and flags precedent for chambers staff, but the requirement for a human judicial officer to weigh credibility and issue binding rulings structurally insulates the bench regardless of AI capability. The real disruption lands on the clerks and researchers who prepare the record a judge reviews, not the judge's own docket.

title: Tutor
insight: Free AI tutors now explain concepts and generate practice problems around the clock at high reliability. Human tutors who survive are the ones providing accountability, emotional support, and executive-function coaching AI can't replicate.

title: Instructional Designer
insight: AI now storyboards and drafts assessments for standard e-learning courses in minutes. Designers who apply learning science, build immersive/simulation experiences, and tie training to business outcomes keep the strategic seat.

title: Corporate Trainer
defense_skill_1: AI Training-Content Pipelines (Synthesia, generative authoring tools)
insight: AI now drafts training decks, quizzes, and completion reports end to end. Trainers who lead live facilitation, drive organizational change, and design AI-upskilling programs are the ones employers still pay for.

title: Academic Advisor
defense_skill_1: AI-Assisted Degree Audit & Advising Platforms (EAB Navigate, Starfish)
insight: AI-assisted degree audits and chatbots now field most routine scheduling and FAQ traffic. Advisors who shift toward career coaching, case management for at-risk students, and the trust-based conversations AI can't fake stay essential.

title: ESL Teacher
defense_skill_1: AI Language-Learning Platforms (adaptive practice, real-time translation)
insight: AI language apps now handle vocabulary drills and pronunciation practice well. ESL teachers who assess real communicative competence and teach cultural context remain the harder-to-automate part of the job.

title: Curriculum Coordinator
insight: AI now handles standards-alignment mapping and pacing-guide drafting quickly. Coordinators who coach teachers through real classroom adoption keep the job's most valuable part.

title: Librarian
insight: AI reference chatbots and discovery tools now field routine lookups and cataloging work. Librarians who teach information literacy, run community programming, and make judgment calls on collections and complex research stay firmly in demand.

title: College Professor
insight: Course prep and first-pass grading increasingly run through AI, but professors who produce original research, run genuine seminar discussion, and mentor graduate students remain hard to displace. The bigger near-term shift is defending academic integrity against AI-written student work.

title: Teacher (K-12)
defense_skill_1: AI Teaching Tools (MagicSchool AI, Khanmigo, Diffit)
insight: AI now drafts lesson plans and grades routine quizzes, freeing time -- but classroom management, mentoring, and differentiated instruction for diverse learners are the parts of teaching AI still can't do.

title: School Counselor
insight: AI can handle scheduling and routine resource lookups, but the crisis intervention, mental-health support, and trust-building at the core of school counseling remain firmly human work.

title: Teaching Assistant
insight: AI can pre-grade quizzes and draft practice worksheets, but the one-on-one support and accommodation help teaching assistants provide inside the classroom is what schools still pay for.

title: School Principal
insight: AI now drafts compliance reports and helps with scheduling logistics, but the accountable, relationship-heavy work of leading a school -- coaching teachers, shaping culture, and handling crises -- stays firmly with the principal.

title: School Psychologist
insight: AI can help draft evaluation-report boilerplate, but clinical interpretation, crisis response, and licensed judgment in IEP meetings keep school psychologists firmly needed.

title: Special Education Teacher
insight: AI can draft IEP paperwork and generate differentiated worksheets, cutting real administrative load. But behavior intervention, physical support, and the legally-mandated human judgment in IEP meetings keep this one of the lowest-displacement roles in education.

title: Preschool Teacher
insight: This is among the most AI-resistant jobs in the index -- hands-on care and safety supervision of very young children sit almost entirely outside current AI capability.

title: Technical Writer
insight: AI now drafts most reference and how-to documentation directly from code and specs, and agent pipelines can keep docs current without a writer opening the file. Technical writers who own information architecture and developer experience across a whole product line are the ones still driving headcount decisions.

title: Translator
insight: Machine translation has closed the quality gap for routine document and localization work to the point that most high-volume translation is now MT-plus-light-editing rather than translate-from-scratch. Translators without certified legal, medical, or literary specialization, or live interpretation skills, are competing directly with a free browser tab.

title: Copy Editor
defense_skill_1: AI-Assisted Editing Tools (Grammarly, LLM Copyedit Passes)
insight: Mechanical copyediting is now handled reliably by AI at commodity cost, making this one of the more exposed roles in the Creative category. Editors doing developmental and high-liability work are the ones still needed.

title: Graphic Designer
insight: AI now produces most first-pass design work in seconds, and agentic pipelines can take a brief through export without a human touch for template-driven formats. Designers who own brand systems, creative direction, and motion work are pulling further ahead of production-only roles.

title: Illustrator
insight: Generative image tools now cover most commercial illustration briefs at production quality, collapsing day-rate stock and concept work. Illustrators building recognizable, ownable style and original IP are the ones still landing licensing and commission deals.

title: Video Editor
insight: AI now assembles rough cuts, captions, and color grades footage without an editor's involvement for routine formats. Editors who shape narrative pacing, run complex composites, and sit with clients through creative decisions are commanding the premium end of the rate card.

title: Journalist
insight: AI now drafts most commodity news competently, and journalists increasingly edit AI output rather than write it from scratch. In an AI-saturated information environment, original reporting and verified sourcing carry a trust premium algorithms can't replicate.

title: Voice Actor
insight: Synthetic voice is now broadcast-quality for narration, IVR, and background game voices, and studios default to it for cost reasons. Voice actors who hold lead roles, union protections, and irreplaceable performance range are the ones still getting booked.

title: Animator
defense_skill_1: Generative Video Tools for Pre-Vis (Runway, Pika)
insight: Generative video tools now handle rough pre-visualization and templated motion graphics, compressing early-stage production time. Animators who own character performance, rigging, and full VFX pipelines remain the load-bearing talent studios build schedules around.

title: Music Producer
defense_skill_1: Generative Music Tools (Suno, Udio, AI Mastering)
insight: Generative tools now produce usable beats and masters in minutes, pulling revenue from commodity production work. Producers who build an artist's sound over a full body of work, and who can run a live session, are the ones labels still pay for.

title: Author
insight: AI can now produce a passable formulaic draft in any popular genre in minutes, flooding self-publishing categories with volume. Authors whose voice, originality, and platform readers actively seek out are the ones still commanding advances and backlist sales.

title: Photographer
insight: Generative image tools have largely displaced commodity stock and product photography. Photographers whose work depends on being physically present — weddings, events, editorial shoots — remain far more insulated than catalog and stock work.

title: Sound Designer
insight: Generative audio tools now cover stock sound effects and basic foley, but the final mix that makes a scene actually land still needs a trained ear. This is a role where AI eats the library work, not the craft.

title: Fashion Designer
insight: AI speeds up print and trend concept work, but a collection still lives or dies on fit, construction, and a designer's point of view. Concept-stage work is commoditizing faster than the craft of making clothes that fit.

title: Screenwriter
insight: AI drafts serviceable coverage and rough outlines fast, but guild rules and the need for a distinct authorial voice keep credited screenwriting largely human for now. That guardrail is a contract fact, not a permanent technical one.

title: Art Director
insight: AI now generates most of the raw creative options an art director reviews, but the judgment call of what actually ships still sits with a human. The job is shifting from making things to curating and directing what AI makes.

title: Interior Designer
insight: AI now speeds up mood boards and concept renders, but the physical realities of a space — code compliance, site conditions, procurement — still require a human designer on-site. Concept visualization is commoditizing faster than the execution work around it.

title: Set Designer
insight: AI speeds up concept pitches and mood boards, but a set designer's real value is running a physical build a director can actually shoot on. That work stays firmly in the physical world.

title: Choreographer
insight: Choreography is one of the most physically embodied creative professions, and current AI has essentially no path into directing a live performer's movement. Administrative and documentation tasks around the craft are the only exposed slice.

title: Craft Artist
insight: Craft art is about as physically embodied as creative work gets, and current AI has no path into the actual making. The only exposed slice is the online-shop marketing layer around the craft.

title: Talent Sourcer
insight: Agentic sourcing tools now run the full search-enrich-outreach loop unattended, which is why this is one of the most exposed jobs in recruiting. Sourcers who survive move into niche market research or into designing the sourcing stack instead of running it by hand.

title: Recruiter
insight: AI now runs the sourcing-to-scheduling pipeline end-to-end for most volume hiring, and agentic tools are starting to run full outreach sequences unattended. Recruiters who survive the cut are the ones selling hard-to-close candidates and running searches AI can't source its way into.

title: Compensation Analyst
defense_skill_1: AI Compensation Analytics Platforms (Payscale, Pave, Mercer AI)
insight: AI now runs most market benchmarking and pay-equity audits directly from survey data, collapsing the analyst hours once spent compiling comp studies. Analysts who move into executive compensation design, equity strategy, and multi-state pay-transparency compliance are absorbing the higher-value work employers still won't hand to a model.

title: Benefits Administrator
insight: AI now runs enrollment, eligibility, and COBRA administration end-to-end. Administrators who move into plan design and vendor negotiation keep the work AI can't yet own.

title: Training Coordinator
defense_skill_1: LMS Administration with AI Scheduling & Reporting
insight: AI now runs the scheduling, tracking, and survey mechanics of a training calendar without a coordinator touching it. The job is narrowing to program design and proving learning ROI, work that determines whether the AI upskilling push actually changes performance.

title: HR Generalist
defense_skill_1: AI HR Chatbot & Case Management Tools (Workday Assistant, Lattice AI)
insight: Chatbots and HRIS assistants now close out most routine FAQ, onboarding, and leave-tracking work that used to fill a generalist's week. The role is consolidating around employee relations, culture, and rolling out the HR tech itself, work that still needs a person in the room.

title: HRIS Analyst
defense_skill_1: HRIS Platform Configuration (Workday, SAP SuccessFactors, UKG)
defense_skill_3: AI Agent Governance in HR Systems
insight: AI copilots now build the routine reports and configuration changes that filled an HRIS analyst's queue. The role is shifting toward system architecture and governing the AI agents now embedded in the platform itself.

title: HR Manager
defense_skill_1: AI HR Platform Administration (Workday AI, Lattice)
insight: AI now drafts the policy documents and runs the routine reporting that used to eat an HR manager's calendar. What's left, and growing, is the part that scales with judgment: culture strategy, thorny employee-relations calls, and writing the rules for how the org itself uses AI.

title: Executive Recruiter
insight: AI compiles the market maps and background research that used to take a search associate weeks, but persuading a sitting CEO to consider a move is not a prompt-able task. Executive recruiters who lean into board relationships and succession advisory are among the least exposed roles in recruiting.

title: Labor Relations Specialist
insight: AI can pull contract clauses and grievance precedent in seconds, but it can't sit across the table in a bargaining session or represent the company in arbitration. This keeps labor relations one of the lowest-exposure specialist roles in HR.

title: Program Coordinator
defense_skill_1: AI Scheduling & Tracker Automation (Asana AI, Notion AI)
defense_skill_3: Agentic AI Literacy for Program Operations
insight: Most of what fills a program coordinator's day, scheduling, tracker updates, minute-taking, is now a background agent task, not a job. Coordinators who move into judgment work, deciding which fire actually needs a human, are the ones who keep a seat.

title: Scrum Master
insight: Burndown charts and retro summaries are now fully automated from tool telemetry, and no one misses making them by hand. That leaves the Scrum Master's real job exposed: can they actually build a team's trust, mediate a conflict, and clear a political impediment? Those who only ran ceremonies are the most at risk.

title: Project Manager
defense_skill_1: AI Project Copilots (Asana AI, Monday AI, MS Project Copilot)
insight: Status decks, timeline updates, and action-item chasing are now a few prompts, not a Friday afternoon. Project managers whose entire job was reporting are being squeezed hardest, while those who negotiate scope, resolve conflict, and keep a team moving through ambiguity are seeing their role expand into program-level trust-building.

title: Product Owner
defense_skill_1: AI Backlog & Story Drafting Tools (Jira AI, Productboard)
defense_skill_3: Agentic AI Literacy for Product Teams
insight: AI now drafts most of the backlog: user stories, acceptance criteria, even release notes, from a short feature brief. That collapses the writing half of the job but sharpens the real one: which ten stories out of a hundred actually get built this sprint. Product Owners who can defend a prioritization call to five stakeholders at once are becoming harder to replace, not easier.

title: Program Manager
defense_skill_1: AI Portfolio & Program Dashboards (Jira Align, Smartsheet AI)
defense_skill_2: Agentic Workflow Governance for Multi-Program Delivery
insight: AI now auto-generates the status rollups and dependency maps that used to eat a program manager's Friday afternoons. What it can't do is decide which of three conflicting program priorities the VP actually cares about, or hold a vendor accountable after a missed milestone. Program managers who move from status-reporting to risk-arbitration are becoming more valuable, not less.

title: Product Manager
defense_skill_1: AI Agent Orchestration for Product Workflows
defense_skill_2: Prompt-Driven PRD and Roadmap Tooling (Productboard AI, Notion AI)
insight: AI now drafts specs and synthesizes user feedback in minutes, collapsing work that used to take a day. That is compressing junior PM headcount at some startups, but it is raising the bar for PMs who can translate ambiguous business goals into hard prioritization calls. The scarcest skill in 2026 isn't writing PRDs, it's deciding which ten features not to build.

title: Technical Program Manager
defense_skill_2: AI-Assisted Dependency & Repo Analytics (GitHub Projects, Jira+Copilot)
defense_skill_3: Agentic AI Literacy for Engineering Programs
insight: AI can already map dependencies across a dozen engineering teams and flag a slipping timeline before a human notices, but it can't sit in a room and get two staff engineers to agree on an architecture trade-off. TPMs whose value is that negotiation, not the status deck, are largely insulated.

title: Engagement Manager
defense_skill_1: AI Contract & SOW Drafting Tools (Ironclad AI)
defense_skill_3: Agentic AI Literacy for Delivery Operations
insight: AI now drafts the status decks and flags slipping deliverables before an engagement manager even opens the project tracker. What still requires a human is reading a client's mood in a steering committee meeting and knowing when to eat a scope change instead of billing for it.

title: Construction Manager
defense_skill_1: AI-assisted scheduling and cost-forecasting platform fluency
insight: Construction management is the highest-exposure role in this category because the job is half digital pipeline: schedules, budgets, RFIs and progress reports are exactly what AI project-management tools now handle, and mid-size and large contractors are already running them. What still requires a human is the part no software owns: reading a job site, negotiating with subcontractors, and making the safety call when the plan and reality diverge.

title: General Contractor
defense_skill_1: AI-assisted bidding and scheduling software fluency
insight: General contractors carry meaningful exposure on the paperwork side, bidding, scheduling and permitting are increasingly software-driven, while site coordination and client relationships remain a human function.

title: Surveyor
defense_skill_1: LiDAR/drone photogrammetry and AI point-cloud processing fluency
defense_skill_2: Professional land surveyor (PLS) licensure and certification
defense_skill_3: Legal boundary and title-dispute consulting skills
insight: Surveying carries the highest exposure among the new trades here: drone and LiDAR data capture with AI point-cloud processing is mature and mainstream, compressing field-crew time, while licensed boundary determination remains a professional, liability-bearing function AI tools only support.

title: CNC Operator
defense_skill_1: CNC programming and CAM software cross-training
defense_skill_2: Multi-machine cell setup and changeover skills
insight: CNC operating is one of the higher-exposure trades in this category: modern machines already run programs and quality checks with minimal input, and adoption is mainstream in manufacturing. The mixed outlook reflects reshoring-driven demand for machine time colliding with fewer operators needed per machine.

title: Home Inspector
defense_skill_3: Client consultation and walkthrough communication skills
insight: Home inspection carries above-average trade exposure because AI image analysis and automated report generation are real, shipped capabilities gaining adoption, while the physical field judgment behind a defect call still needs a person on site.

title: Machinist
insight: Machinists face moderate, rising exposure as CAM and AI toolpath tools take over programming, but precision setup and tolerance troubleshooting on the actual floor still require a trained eye AI inspection cameras don't fully replace yet.

title: Auto Mechanic
insight: Auto mechanics carry more exposure than most trades because AI-assisted diagnostic platforms are mature, mainstream in dealership service bays, and genuinely automate fault-code interpretation. The mixed trend reflects two forces pulling apart: EV and ADAS complexity is creating higher-skill demand even as routine ICE maintenance keeps shrinking with vehicle reliability and diagnostic automation.

title: Appliance Repair Technician
defense_skill_1: Smart-appliance and IoT diagnostic platform fluency
insight: Appliance repair shows moderate exposure as manufacturer diagnostic apps mature, but the trend is mixed: smart appliances create more diagnostic complexity even as consumers increasingly replace rather than repair low-cost units.

title: Solar Installer
defense_skill_2: AI solar-design and permitting software fluency
defense_skill_3: Electrification and energy-consulting skills for customers
insight: Solar installation shows one of the fastest tool-maturity curves in the trades: AI-driven design and permitting software is now close to standard practice, automating the proposal-to-permit pipeline. The physical install, especially battery and microgrid commissioning as storage attaches to more systems, is where the job's growth and its floor against automation both sit.

title: Diesel Mechanic
defense_skill_1: Fleet telematics and predictive-maintenance platform fluency
insight: Diesel mechanics see moderate exposure because fleet telematics and AI-assisted diagnostics are mature and mainstream among large carriers, automating first-level fault triage, while the physical repair work and DOT-compliance judgment stay firmly manual.

title: HVAC Technician
defense_skill_3: EPA refrigerant handling and compliance expertise
insight: HVAC sits slightly above other trades because connected thermostats and AI-assisted diagnostics genuinely automate first-level fault triage today, and adoption is real at scale among larger service franchises. The heat-pump and electrification wave is the growth lane: commissioning new all-electric systems isn't something diagnostic software can do end-to-end.

title: Landscaper
defense_skill_1: AI landscape-design and route-optimization software fluency
insight: Landscaping's risk is modest and concentrated in scheduling and design proposals, where AI route-optimization and rendering tools are gaining real traction, while planting and hardscape installation stay physical work.

title: Locksmith
insight: Locksmithing's risk is modest and shifting: mechanical lockout work stays entirely manual, while growth in smart-lock and access-control system installation is reshaping the job's digital half.

title: Wind Turbine Technician
defense_skill_1: Advanced turbine platform and gearbox certification
insight: Wind turbine technician remains one of the fastest-growing trades: remote condition-monitoring is mature and near-universal among operators, but the physical climb-and-repair work keeps risk low even as demand rises with fleet growth.

title: Electrician
defense_skill_1: EV charger and smart-panel installation certification
defense_skill_2: AI-assisted estimating and takeoff software fluency
insight: Electricians sit near the floor of AI displacement risk because the licensed physical work of pulling wire and passing inspection has no automatable substitute, but the back office is changing fast: AI-assisted estimating and permit-paperwork tools are already trimming hours from bidding. The growth lane runs through electrification work AI can't yet spec: EV chargers, smart panels and solar-ready service upgrades.

title: Heavy Equipment Operator
defense_skill_1: GPS machine-control and semi-autonomous grading certification
defense_skill_3: Fleet telemetry and maintenance-tracking software literacy
insight: Heavy-equipment operation trends toward rising demand as infrastructure spending grows, and semi-autonomous GPS grading systems are shifting the job toward oversight of assisted equipment rather than replacing operators outright.

title: Carpenter
defense_skill_1: Digital takeoff and cut-list software fluency
defense_skill_2: Finish carpentry and custom fabrication specialization
insight: Carpentry's risk is capped by the physical craft: framing, trim and site-adapted problem-solving resist automation, while digital cut-list and takeoff tools quietly speed up the estimating side of the job. Carpenters who can run that software and lead multi-trade sequencing are the best-positioned in the trade.

title: Elevator Technician
insight: Elevator technicians see rising demand as aging buildings require modernization, and remote predictive-maintenance sensors now handle real monitoring work, but code-compliant repair itself remains licensed, hands-on work.

title: Painter
insight: Painting's exposure is modest and concentrated in bidding: AI photo-to-estimate tools are gaining real traction with larger companies, while surface prep and application stay a hands-on craft.

title: Construction Laborer
defense_skill_1: Apprenticeship pathway into a licensed trade
defense_skill_3: Site logistics and material-tracking software literacy
insight: Construction laborer is near the floor of this index: the job is site presence and physical handling, and the only digital slice, delivery and inventory tracking, is a small share of the role.

title: Crane Operator
defense_skill_1: Advanced crane certification (NCCCO, larger load classes)
defense_skill_2: Load-monitoring and anti-collision sensor system fluency
insight: Crane operation stays low-risk: sensor-based load monitoring is mature and mainstream on large sites, but the safety-critical judgment of an actual lift has no autonomous substitute in production use.

title: Drywall Installer
defense_skill_1: Level-5 finish and specialty texture specialization
insight: Drywall installation carries low risk: hanging, taping and finishing remain manual craft, with automation limited to the material takeoff and bidding side of larger commercial jobs.

title: Flooring Installer
defense_skill_2: Digital measurement and takeoff software fluency
insight: Flooring installation carries low risk: the craft itself resists automation, with AI tools reaching only the room-measurement and quoting step ahead of the job.

title: Insulation Worker
insight: Insulation work stays low-risk on the install side while energy-code tightening and retrofit incentives drive rising demand, with AI energy-modeling tools automating only the estimating step.

title: Roofer
defense_skill_1: Drone/AI roof-inspection and measurement software fluency
insight: Roofing stays low-risk because the physical work at height has no substitute, though drone-based inspection and AI measurement tools are starting to automate the estimating step for larger companies.

title: Welder
insight: Field welding stays low-risk because robotic welding, though mature, is confined to fixed factory cells and hasn't reached one-off structural and site work. The exposure that exists is administrative: certification tracking and inspection logs, not the weld itself.

title: Glazier
insight: Glazing is a low-risk trade: precision glass installation has no automated substitute, and AI tooling in the field is essentially absent beyond generic estimating software.

title: Mason
defense_skill_3: Digital takeoff and estimating software literacy
insight: Masonry is among the lowest-risk trades: robotic bricklaying remains a pilot-stage novelty, and the craft's precision work stays entirely manual.

title: Ironworker
defense_skill_1: Advanced rigging and high-rise erection certification
insight: Ironwork sits at the floor of this index: structural steel erection at height is inherently manual and safety-critical, and demand is rising with the data-center and infrastructure construction boom.

title: Plumber
defense_skill_2: AI-assisted scheduling and dispatch software fluency
insight: Plumbing is one of the lowest-risk trades in the index: licensed, hands-in-the-wall work has no automatable substitute, and even the back office runs mostly on phone calls rather than software. What is changing is dispatch and invoicing, where AI scheduling tools are starting to cut administrative overhead for larger service franchises.

title: Shipping Clerk
defense_skill_3: Vendor/carrier coordination for damaged or lost freight
insight: This is one of the more exposed roles in the category: manifest generation, labeling, and inventory reconciliation are exactly the kind of digital record-processing work agentic tools already own end to end. Clerks who move toward exception handling and vendor coordination are furthest from the automated core.

title: Freight Broker
defense_skill_1: Digital freight marketplace and AI pricing-tool fluency
insight: Freight brokerage is one of the most exposed roles in this category because it's already a digital-marketplace business: standard load matching, quoting, and booking are agent-ownable today. Brokers who survive are shifting toward the relationship-heavy, hard-to-cover freight that automated marketplaces still struggle to fill.

title: Dispatcher
defense_skill_1: AI dispatch and TMS platform oversight
insight: Dispatch is one of the more exposed roles in this category precisely because it's a digital coordination job, not a physical one: routine matching, scheduling, and status updates are already agent-handled at many large fleets. The dispatchers who remain valuable are the ones running exception queues AI escalates to them, not the ones doing standard matching by hand.

title: Customs Broker
defense_skill_1: AI-assisted trade-compliance and HTS classification tools
defense_skill_2: Regulatory audit defense and penalty mitigation
insight: Classification and filing are exactly the kind of rules-based digital work AI already handles well, which is pushing routine entry work toward automation. The licensed judgment and liability side of the job, defending an audit, resolving an ambiguous classification, is what keeps this role from moving further, faster.

title: Logistics Manager
insight: AI has taken over much of the reporting and route-optimization work that used to fill a logistics manager's week, but the vendor negotiation, team leadership, and judgment calls during disruptions remain squarely human, keeping overall risk moderate rather than high.

title: Transportation Planner
defense_skill_1: AI-assisted traffic simulation and modeling tools
insight: AI modeling tools speed up the data-analysis side of transportation planning, but the public engagement and political trade-off work, deciding whose neighborhood gets the new transit line, remains a human judgment call agents don't reach.

title: Package Handler
insight: Major parcel hubs already run mature automated sortation, which has shifted headcount toward loading, unloading, and exception handling rather than eliminating it outright; smaller regional facilities lag well behind.

title: Forklift Operator
defense_skill_1: AGV/AMR fleet coexistence and safety operation
insight: Autonomous forklifts are a mature product, but they are best at repetitive, mapped-route moves; the irregular loads and mixed human/robot floors of most warehouses still need a licensed human operator, so adoption is real but concentrated in a minority of large, newer facilities.

title: Warehouse Worker
defense_skill_3: Quality control and safety inspection on automated lines
insight: Large e-commerce and 3PL fulfillment centers already run substantial robotics, but that has mostly shifted the job toward robot-adjacent work (loading, exception handling, machine oversight) rather than eliminating headcount outright. The smaller, non-automated warehouse base, still the majority of facilities, faces materially less near-term pressure.

title: Rideshare Driver
defense_skill_2: Operating in non-geofenced, unmapped service areas
defense_skill_3: Platform and gig-app fluency across multiple services
insight: Robotaxis are commercially live today, not a future prospect, but confined to specific mapped cities and conditions. Drivers in those metro robotaxi zones face real and growing displacement over the next few years; drivers everywhere else face materially less pressure until geofences expand, which has historically happened city by city, slowly.

title: Delivery Driver
defense_skill_3: Customer-facing problem resolution for failed deliveries
insight: Drone and sidewalk-robot delivery are commercially operating today, but only in select metro pilot zones covering a small fraction of last-mile volume nationally. The realistic medium-term shift is fewer stops per driver as robots absorb easy, short-hop drops, with humans increasingly routed to the access-restricted and exception-heavy deliveries robots can't yet handle.

title: Courier
insight: Robot and drone courier pilots exist in a handful of metro markets but cover a narrow slice of courier volume; the specialized, time-sensitive document runs (medical, legal) that make up much of this role's value are not close to automatable.

title: Truck Driver
defense_skill_2: Fleet telematics and AI dispatch-system fluency
insight: Autonomous trucking is real but narrow: driverless freight runs on a handful of mapped interstate lanes today, not on the docks, city streets, and weather a driver actually navigates. The near-term threat is on the paperwork side (logs, routing, load matching), already largely automated, while the medium-term pressure on long-haul lanes will build as mapped corridors expand over the next several years.

title: Postal Worker
insight: Facility-level sorting has been automated for years without eliminating the carrier role, because the door-to-door delivery route itself remains physical work automation hasn't reached; near-term risk stays low.

title: Train Conductor
defense_skill_1: Rail safety inspection and incident response
defense_skill_2: Automated signaling and PTC system fluency
defense_skill_3: Yard and crew coordination during disruptions
insight: Rail automation has focused on signaling and braking systems, not on replacing the conductor's physical safety and coordination duties; driverless freight rail trials exist but remain narrow pilots years from broad deployment.

title: Aircraft Mechanic
defense_skill_2: Complex diagnostic and structural repair certification
insight: AI predictive-maintenance tools are shipping and genuinely useful for flagging what needs attention, but the licensed, hands-on repair and airworthiness certification at the center of this role remain firmly human work.

title: Bus Driver
insight: Autonomous shuttle pilots are real but confined to fixed, low-speed routes; mixed-traffic transit and intercity bus driving, with the safety and liability obligations of carrying passengers, is not close to automatable at production reliability.

title: Air Traffic Controller
defense_skill_2: AI decision-support tool oversight and override judgment
insight: Controllers already work alongside mature automation aids, but the FAA has not reduced staffing because final separation authority stays with a certified human; near-term risk is low, concentrated instead in tool-assisted efficiency gains rather than headcount reduction.

title: Airline Pilot
defense_skill_3: Crew resource management and safety leadership
insight: Autopilot already handles most of the flying hours in a commercial flight, and that has been true for years without displacing pilots, because certification, insurance, and passenger trust require a licensed human in command. Reduced-crew and single-pilot concepts are in FAA-monitored study, not deployment; a real regulatory shift is possible over a decade-plus horizon but is not the near-term picture.

title: Flight Attendant
defense_skill_1: Emergency response and evacuation command certification
insight: This is an in-person safety role first and a service role second; current AI has no path to performing either at the required reliability, so displacement risk stays low regardless of how much AI improves in adjacent booking and scheduling systems.

title: Cashier
defense_skill_1: Self-checkout exception handling and loss-prevention judgment
defense_skill_2: Customer-service recovery on the retail floor
defense_skill_3: Cross-training into inventory or fulfillment tech roles
insight: Self-checkout has already automated the core transaction, and most large retailers now treat it as the default lane, not the exception. What remains for cashiers is what the machines can't handle: age-verification disputes, theft prevention, and the returns a kiosk kicks back to a human.

title: Travel Agent
defense_skill_2: AI trip-planning tool integration into client service
insight: AI trip planners already out-research most human agents on a standard beach vacation, comparing hundreds of fares and itineraries in seconds. The agents still working are the ones handling what breaks: a canceled flight mid-trip, a ten-stop corporate itinerary, or a client who wants judgment, not just options.

title: Hotel Front Desk Agent
defense_skill_2: Hotel PMS and AI check-in system administration
defense_skill_3: Cross-training toward guest-relations or revenue roles
insight: Mobile check-in and keyless entry already handle the routine arrival for most guests. The desk agent's value has shifted to the guests self-service can't handle: the VIP request, the overbooking problem, the complaint that needs a human decision.

title: Fast Food Worker
defense_skill_1: Working alongside AI drive-thru and kiosk ordering systems
defense_skill_3: Cross-training into shift-lead or scheduling roles
insight: AI voice ordering is already live at drive-thru windows for several national chains, taking over the exact scripted exchange this job used to require. What's left is assembly speed and handling the orders the AI gets wrong.

title: Concierge
defense_skill_2: AI concierge chatbot oversight and escalation management
defense_skill_3: Local vendor relationship-building for exclusive access
insight: AI concierge chatbots now handle the reservation-and-recommendation baseline well enough that guests rarely notice. The role has shifted toward the requests a chatbot escalates: the impossible booking, the emergency, the guest who wants a person, not a menu of options.

title: Barista
defense_skill_1: Specialty beverage craft and latte-art skill
insight: Mobile order-ahead already handles the ordering conversation, and a handful of robotic kiosks now pull shots too. The craft differentiation, latte art, dialing in espresso, building cafe regulars, is exactly what keeps human baristas ahead of a vending machine.

title: Restaurant Manager
defense_skill_1: AI scheduling and inventory platform fluency
insight: Scheduling and inventory software now generate the labor and ordering numbers a manager used to build by hand. The job is shifting toward what the software can't do: coaching staff and fixing a bad guest experience in real time.

title: Event Planner
defense_skill_1: On-site crisis management for live events
defense_skill_2: AI budgeting and vendor-matching tool fluency
defense_skill_3: Client vision translation and stakeholder management
insight: AI can already shortlist vendors and track a budget against invoices. It cannot fix a collapsed tent an hour before guests arrive, which is still the job most clients are actually paying for.

title: Security Guard
defense_skill_1: AI video-analytics platform operation and alert triage
insight: AI camera analytics already does the watching, flagging anomalies across hundreds of feeds no human could track continuously. It still can't walk the floor, de-escalate a confrontation, or make the judgment call on when to intervene, which is why the job is shifting from watching monitors to responding to what the monitors flag.

title: Hotel Manager
defense_skill_1: AI revenue-management and dynamic-pricing platform fluency
insight: Dynamic pricing algorithms now set most room rates automatically. What a hotel manager still owns is running the property when something goes wrong, staffing gaps, VIP escalations, brand-standard enforcement, none of which the pricing engine touches.

title: Tour Guide
defense_skill_3: Group-management and safety judgment on the move
