insight: An AI audio guide can narrate a landmark competently for a fraction of the cost. What it can't do is read a group's energy, take a real question off-script, or turn a rained-out afternoon into a good story anyway.

title: Personal Trainer
defense_skill_2: AI fitness-app integration into client programming
insight: AI apps now generate a competent generic workout plan for free. What clients still pay a trainer for is what an app can't do in the room: catching bad form before it causes an injury and providing the accountability that keeps people showing up.

title: Restaurant Server
defense_skill_2: Working alongside AI ordering and payment kiosks
insight: QR ordering and tableside tablets have already automated the mechanical half of the job: taking orders and running payment. What's left is exactly what pays the tips: reading a table, recovering from a bad moment, and making a stranger feel looked after.

title: Line Cook
defense_skill_1: Multi-station speed and consistency under service pressure
defense_skill_2: Kitchen inventory and prep-planning tool fluency
defense_skill_3: Cross-training toward sous chef or kitchen management
insight: Single-station robotics (fry baskets, portioning arms) have entered a handful of high-volume kitchens, but running a full line during a dinner rush is still beyond what any deployed system handles end to end.

title: Chef
defense_skill_3: Kitchen team leadership under live service pressure
insight: Line cooking and plating still require dexterity and split-second judgment no kitchen robot handles at scale, so chefs stay largely insulated. The exposure sits in the office: AI now drafts prep lists, forecasts food cost, and reorders inventory, shrinking the admin half of the job faster than the culinary half.

title: Funeral Director
defense_skill_1: Grief support and family counseling skills
defense_skill_2: Licensing and continuing education in mortuary science
defense_skill_3: Digital paperwork and permit-filing tool fluency
insight: The paperwork around death, permits, certificates, scheduling, is genuinely automatable and slowly is being automated. Nothing about supporting a grieving family through a funeral is, and that's the part of the job that was never really about paperwork anyway.

title: Bartender
defense_skill_1: Craft cocktail development and flavor innovation
defense_skill_3: POS and tab-automation fluency for high-volume service
insight: Robotic bar kiosks are a novelty act, not a threat: mixing well drinks at volume while reading a room's mood and pace is still squarely human work. The real automation is in the back end, tab tracking and pour reconciliation, not behind the bar.

title: Esthetician
defense_skill_2: AI skin-analysis and diagnostic tool fluency
insight: AI imaging can now flag skin concerns and suggest a regimen during intake, but delivering the actual treatment, and judging contraindications on a real client, still requires a licensed, hands-on professional.

title: Barber
defense_skill_1: Precision cutting and traditional shave technique
defense_skill_2: AI booking and client-management app fluency
insight: No AI tool has come close to performing a haircut or straight-razor shave. Exposure here is confined entirely to scheduling and admin, not the chair.

title: Hair Stylist
defense_skill_1: Advanced cutting and color technique specialization
defense_skill_2: AI virtual try-on and style-consultation tools
insight: Virtual try-on apps can preview a haircut; no product can perform one. The stylist's exposure is entirely in scheduling and formula recordkeeping, not the chair.

title: Janitor
insight: Autonomous scrubbers already handle open floor space in big-box stores and warehouses overnight. Everything irregular, restrooms, offices, spills, hazards, still needs a person, and increasingly that person also supervises the robot.

title: Childcare Worker
insight: Nothing about supervising and comforting a room of toddlers is automatable at scale, so the core job is essentially untouched. Adoption shows up only in the paperwork around it: attendance logs, meal records, and parent updates.

title: Housekeeper
defense_skill_2: Task-management app fluency for room assignments
insight: No deployed robot handles the variability of a real guest room, scattered belongings, different layouts, spills. Automation touches only the scheduling around the job, not the cleaning itself.

title: Pet Groomer
defense_skill_2: Safe handling of anxious or aggressive animals
defense_skill_3: AI booking and client-reminder app fluency
insight: No automated station can safely handle an anxious, wriggling animal through a full groom. The job's small digital footprint, booking and reminders, is where its modest exposure is concentrated.

title: Massage Therapist
defense_skill_1: Specialized therapeutic technique (sports, prenatal, medical massage)
insight: Therapeutic touch calibrated to an individual body and injury has no automated substitute today. The role's small digital footprint, booking and intake, is where nearly all of its modest exposure lives.

title: Statistician
insight: Agentic notebooks now run entire analysis pipelines from cleaning to draft reporting. Statisticians who own study design, causal reasoning, and validity auditing of AI-generated results are moving from technician to the analysis's final authority.

title: Quality Engineer
insight: AI vision systems and SPC dashboards now catch defects and flag anomalies automatically, but tracing the anomaly back to its physical cause on the line still needs a quality engineer walking the floor.

title: Industrial Engineer
insight: Simulation-driven line balancing and scheduling now run largely on their own. Industrial engineers who own operator-facing change management and ergonomics design keep the roles AI can't run unsupervised.

title: Epidemiologist
defense_skill_3: Novel Study Design for Emerging Threats
insight: Surveillance data pipelines and forecasting models increasingly run themselves, but translating a cluster of numbers into a policy recommendation the public will trust remains an epidemiologist's job.

title: Materials Scientist
insight: AI-driven composition screening now narrows candidate materials before a single sample is made. Materials scientists who master physical synthesis and failure analysis on real parts stay essential downstream of the algorithm.

title: Meteorologist
insight: AI forecast models now rival traditional physics-based models on routine forecasts, but the decision to issue a tornado warning, and the words used to communicate it, still rest with a meteorologist.

title: Electrical Engineer
insight: Layout, routing, and compliance paperwork are increasingly automated by AI-assisted EDA tools. Electrical engineers who specialize in power-system architecture, safety-critical validation, and system integration hold the ground AI can't reach yet.

title: Chemist
insight: AI can now suggest reaction routes and read routine spectra, but someone still has to run the bench, handle the hazards, and judge when a novel result is real. That physical and hypothesis-generation work keeps chemists in the lab.

title: Mechanical Engineer
insight: AI now runs simulation sweeps and drafts CAD variants overnight, compressing the early design loop. Mechanical engineers who own physical validation, manufacturability trade-offs, and final design sign-off stay indispensable.

title: Biologist
insight: Genomic analysis and literature synthesis are increasingly AI-assisted, but fieldwork and wet-lab experimentation still need a biologist's hands and judgment on the actual organism.

title: Biomedical Engineer
insight: AI drafts routine submission sections and runs device simulations, but clinical validation and regulatory risk sign-off still require an engineer to own patient safety.

title: Physicist
insight: Simulation and data reduction increasingly run on AI-assisted pipelines, but designing the experiment and formulating the theory behind it remain the physicist's irreplaceable core.

title: Research Scientist
insight: AI research assistants now draft literature reviews and propose experiment variants, but deciding which hypothesis is worth testing, and validating the result, is still a scientist's call.

title: Environmental Engineer
insight: Permit drafting and dispersion modeling are increasingly AI-assisted, but remediation strategy and agency negotiation still depend on an engineer's field judgment and standing with regulators.

title: Civil Engineer
insight: AI now handles quantity takeoffs and BIM clash checks, but stamped drawings still carry an engineer's legal liability. Civil engineers who lean into field inspection and public-project stakeholder work stay ahead of the automation curve.

title: Chemical Engineer
insight: Simulation sweeps and balance calculations increasingly run themselves, but hazard analysis and scale-up decisions still ride on an engineer's judgment. Chemical engineers who own plant safety leadership hold the highest-value ground.

title: Aerospace Engineer
insight: Design-iteration cycles are compressing as AI-assisted CFD and structural tools mature, but certification and airworthiness sign-off remain tightly regulated, engineer-owned work that AI cannot touch.

title: Structural Engineer
insight: Load-path analysis and calc-package drafting are increasingly AI-assisted, but the stamp on a structural drawing, and the legal liability it carries, still belongs to a licensed engineer.

title: Geologist
insight: AI now chews through seismic and satellite datasets fast, but reading an actual outcrop or core sample in the field is still a geologist's call. Fieldwork keeps this role grounded, literally.

title: Petroleum Engineer
insight: Reservoir simulation and drilling-data analysis increasingly run on AI-assisted models, but a well-control decision at the rig site still needs a petroleum engineer who owns the physical risk.

title: Tax Examiner
defense_skill_1: Automated matching and fraud-detection system fluency
defense_skill_3: Taxpayer dispute resolution and communication skills
insight: One of the most exposed public-sector roles here: matching and flagging is exactly the routine digital work automation already handles well.

title: Court Clerk
defense_skill_1: E-filing and court case-management system fluency
defense_skill_3: Public-facing customer service and courtroom support skills
insight: A document-heavy government role where mature e-filing and OCR tools already cover much of the routine workload.

title: Policy Analyst
defense_skill_1: AI research-synthesis and drafting tool fluency
insight: One of the highest-automation roles in this category: the drafting and research pipeline is squarely inside what agentic tools do well today.

title: Urban Planner
defense_skill_1: AI-assisted GIS and scenario-modeling tool fluency
defense_skill_2: Public engagement and stakeholder facilitation skills
insight: The analytical half of planning is automating steadily; the public-hearing, political-negotiation half is not.

title: 911 Dispatcher
defense_skill_1: Next-gen 911 (NG911) and AI-assisted CAD platform fluency
defense_skill_3: High-stress call triage and de-escalation skills
insight: Dispatch is one of the most digitally automatable public-safety jobs on this index, and NG911 tooling is already mature. What keeps adoption slow is money and liability, not capability: the gap between a well-funded metro center and a rural county center is enormous.

title: Building Inspector
defense_skill_3: On-site inspection and client communication skills
insight: Plan-check paperwork is a good fit for automated compliance review; the physical walkthrough and sign-off liability are not.

title: Detective
defense_skill_1: AI-assisted digital forensics and case-linkage tools
defense_skill_2: Advanced interview and interrogation technique certification
insight: Records work is automating fast, but the interview room and the witness stand still require a human.

title: Probation Officer
defense_skill_1: Risk-assessment algorithm interpretation and case-management software
insight: Risk-scoring tools already do a chunk of the analytic work here; the supervisory relationship is what remains distinctly human.

title: Emergency Management Director
insight: Planning paperwork automates readily; the job's value is in the live coordination no model can run during an actual disaster.

title: Social Worker
insight: The paperwork half of social work is getting real automation help, which is good news if it frees caseworkers for the in-person visits and trust-building that no tool can substitute for. Agencies that adopt these tools fastest are still the exception, not the rule, in the public sector.

title: City Manager
defense_skill_3: Council relations and public communication skills
insight: An executive role where AI helps with reporting mechanics but not the accountability and relationship work that defines the job.

title: Police Officer
insight: Patrol work itself sits outside AI's reach, but the paperwork half of the job is being rewritten: departments that adopted AI report-drafting tools report officers spending far less time behind a keyboard. Officers who master those tools free up the in-person hours that actually define the job.

title: Correctional Officer
defense_skill_2: Facility surveillance and behavior-monitoring system literacy
insight: One of the most physically-grounded roles in this index; AI's reach is limited to logging and monitoring at the margins.

title: Park Ranger
defense_skill_1: Wildlife-monitoring analytics and camera-trap data tools
insight: Among the least automatable roles in this index; the job exists precisely where digital tools don't reach.

title: Firefighter
defense_skill_1: Technical rescue and hazmat response certification
defense_skill_2: Incident command software and predictive-dispatch tool literacy
insight: Firefighting sits at the physical floor of this index: suppression and rescue can't be automated at any reliability AI offers today. The only real AI pressure is on the administrative tail of the job.
